content.text: React distinguishes two kinds of renders: the
that mounts a component tree into the DOM for the first time, and
that update an already-mounted tree when state, props, or context change. They share the same Trigger → Render → Commit pipeline, but differ in what React does with the output at each stage.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of initial render and re-render flows
attrs.title: Initial render creates the full DOM; re-renders patch only what changed

content.text: The initial render happens exactly once per React root, triggered by calling
. React has no previous tree to compare against, so it calls every component in the tree for the first time and builds the DOM from scratch.
Every component function is called once, depth-first from the root.
State is initialized to the value passed to
setup functions run (there is no previous cleanup to call).

After the initial mount, any state change, prop change, or context value change schedules a re-render of the affected component and its descendants. React calls the relevant component functions again, diffs the new element tree against the previous one, and patches only the DOM nodes that actually changed.
Calls the component function again with its current state and new props.
Diffs the returned element tree against the previous output.
Applies only the minimal set of DOM mutations needed — unchanged nodes are not touched.
Preserves state for any component whose type and position in the tree match the previous render.
The component that owns the state + its descendants
Skipped if new value === old value
Skipped if reducer returns same reference
Regardless of how far down the tree
What does NOT trigger a re-render
— refs are mutable and intentionally bypass React's render cycle.
Setting state to the same value — React uses
to compare the old and new values and bails out if they are identical.
Regular JavaScript variable mutations — mutating a local variable or an object without calling a setter is invisible to React.
A re-render calls your component function again, but it does not reset state. React ties state to the component's
, not to the function call. The state from the previous render is retrieved and passed back into hooks via React's internal fiber structure.
When a component is removed from the tree (unmounted), React destroys its state entirely. If the same component type mounts again at the same position, React initializes its state fresh from the
How React Identifies Components Across Renders
React matches components between renders by their
. If the same component type renders at the same position, React re-uses its state. If the type changes, React unmounts the old component (destroying state) and mounts a new one.
To preserve state despite conditional rendering at the same position, keep the component type stable and pass an

By default, when a component re-renders, all of its children re-render too — even if their props did not change. This is usually fast enough because re-rendering just calls a function and React's diff is O(n), but it can become a bottleneck for large trees with expensive renders.
prop is a React element created by the
parent. If the wrapper component re-renders but the element passed as
was created in a component that did not re-render, the child will not re-render either.
Effect Behavior: Initial Mount vs Updates

Effects behave differently on the initial render than on updates. The dependency array controls when effects run, but the cleanup lifecycle only fires on updates and unmounts — never on the initial mount.
Rendering vs Painting: A Subtle Distinction

"Rendering" in React means calling component functions to produce an element tree. "Painting" means the browser drawing pixels. React rendering does not necessarily produce a visible change — a re-render that produces the same output as the previous render may result in zero DOM mutations and zero browser repaints.

A component can re-render many times without the browser repainting, if React determines that the DOM does not need to change. Re-rendering is just calling a function — it is only expensive if the function itself is expensive or if the tree is enormous.
React DevTools makes it easy to see which renders are initial mounts vs updates:
tab, enable "Record why each component rendered". A component will show "First rendered" on mount and the specific state or prop name on subsequent renders.
tab, enable "Highlight updates when components render" to see a flash on every re-render.
Components highlighted in red in the Profiler rendered longest; green means fast.
Object reference unchanged — no re-render triggered
Spread into a new object before calling setter
Change the key prop to force a remount
New type every render — unmounts and remounts, losing state
Children re-render by default when parent does
Forcing a Re-render: The key Prop Reset

prop is the primary mechanism to force a component to remount, resetting its state completely. This is useful when you want to discard and reinitialize a component whenever some identity value changes.
, navigating from user A to user B would keep the
guarantees a clean initial render for every new user.
Before scheduling a re-render, React uses
to compare the new state value with the current one. If they are the same reference or the same primitive value, React skips the re-render entirely.
The implication for objects: even if the data is logically the same, creating a new object triggers a re-render. Unnecessarily spreading state (e.g.,
when nothing changed) causes wasteful re-renders.
React 18 assigns priority to updates. Not all re-renders are treated equally:
(typing, clicking) are rendered synchronously to feel instant.
(large list filters, route changes) can be interrupted if a higher-priority update arrives.
run when the browser is idle, via
Re-rendering Does Not Always Change the DOM

A common misconception is that every re-render updates the DOM. The render phase produces an element tree, and the commit phase applies only the changes that differ from the previous tree. If a re-render produces identical JSX, React may apply zero DOM mutations for that subtree.

Re-rendering is usually cheap (just a function call and a diff). DOM mutations can be more expensive. Optimize re-render frequency when the component function itself is costly; optimize DOM patches only if profiling shows React is applying mutations unnecessarily.
In React Testing Library, the initial render maps to
or user events that trigger state changes:
The initial render runs all components for the first time, builds the DOM from scratch, and fires
Re-renders call only the affected component and its descendants, patch the DOM minimally, and run effect cleanup + setup only when dependencies change.
State persists across re-renders but is destroyed on unmount. Changing a
forces a remount and resets state.
React identifies components by type and tree position — switching the type at the same position destroys the old state.
Children re-render by default when their parent re-renders.
